title: Backup & Recovery
description: Backup strategies, restore operations, and recovery models

concepts.title: Database Backups

sqlServer.code:
-- Full Database Backup
BACKUP DATABASE AdventureWorks2019
TO DISK = 'C:\Backups\AdventureWorks2019_Full.bak'
WITH COMPRESSION,
     CHECKSUM,
     STATS = 10;

-- Differential Backup
BACKUP DATABASE AdventureWorks2019
TO DISK = 'C:\Backups\AdventureWorks2019_Diff.bak'
WITH DIFFERENTIAL,
     COMPRESSION,
     CHECKSUM;

-- Transaction Log Backup
BACKUP LOG AdventureWorks2019
TO DISK = 'C:\Backups\AdventureWorks2019_Log.trn'
WITH CHECKSUM;

-- Multiple backup devices for parallel operations
BACKUP DATABASE AdventureWorks2019
TO DISK = 'C:\Backups\AW_1.bak',
   DISK = 'C:\Backups\AW_2.bak',
   DISK = 'C:\Backups\AW_3.bak'
WITH COMPRESSION,
     FORMAT,
     INIT;
sqlServer.description:
SQL Server provides three backup types:
                • Full backups capture the entire database
                • Differential backups capture changes since last full backup
                • Log backups capture transaction log records for point-in-time recovery

                Key features:
                • Built-in compression reduces backup size by 50-70%
                • CHECKSUM validates backup integrity during backup and restore
                • Multiple backup devices support parallel operations for large databases
                • STATS option shows progress during backup operations

                Always use CHECKSUM for backup validation. Compression is highly recommended for all backups. Full backups are required before differential backups can be taken. Log backups require FULL or BULK_LOGGED recovery model. Use multiple devices for faster backup/restore on large databases.

postgres.code:
-- Full Database Backup (Plain SQL format)
pg_dump -h localhost -U postgres -d adventureworks \
        -f adventureworks_backup.sql

-- Custom Format Backup (Compressed, recommended)
pg_dump -h localhost -U postgres -d adventureworks \
        -Fc -f adventureworks_backup.custom

-- Directory Format (Parallel processing)
pg_dump -h localhost -U postgres -d adventureworks \
        -Fd -j 4 -f backup_directory/

-- Compressed Plain Text Backup
pg_dump -h localhost -U postgres -d adventureworks \
        | gzip > adventureworks_backup.sql.gz

-- Schema-only Backup
pg_dump -h localhost -U postgres -d adventureworks \
        --schema-only -f schema_backup.sql

-- Data-only Backup
pg_dump -h localhost -U postgres -d adventureworks \
        --data-only -f data_backup.sql

-- Exclude specific tables
pg_dump -h localhost -U postgres -d adventureworks \
        -Fc --exclude-table=audit_log \
        -f adventureworks_no_audit.custom
postgres.description:
PostgreSQL uses pg_dump utility for logical backups with multiple format options:
                • Plain format (-Fp): Standard SQL commands, human-readable
                • Custom format (-Fc): Compressed binary format, fastest restore, recommended
                • Directory format (-Fd): Multiple files, supports parallel restore
                • Tar format (-Ft): Tar archive format

                Key features:
                • Parallel dumping with -j option dramatically improves performance
                • Built-in compression with custom format reduces backup size
                • Supports schema-only, data-only, or full database backups
                • Fine-grained control over what to include/exclude
                • Transaction-consistent snapshots without blocking writers

                Custom format (-Fc) is recommended for most scenarios. Use directory format (-Fd) for very large databases with parallel restore. pg_dump creates consistent snapshots without locking database. For physical backups and WAL archiving, use pg_basebackup. Parallel dumps significantly reduce backup time on multi-core systems.

concepts.keyDifference: External utility vs SQL command - no T-SQL equivalent. Multiple output formats vs single .bak format. Logical backup (SQL statements) vs physical backup (pages). No built-in differential backup - full backups only. Parallel processing available with directory format. Can backup specific schemas, tables, or exclude objects. No transaction log backups - use WAL archiving instead.

concepts.title: Database Restores

sqlServer.code:
-- Full Database Restore
RESTORE DATABASE AdventureWorks2019_Test
FROM DISK = 'C:\Backups\AdventureWorks2019_Full.bak'
WITH REPLACE,
     CHECKSUM,
     STATS = 10;

-- Restore with file relocation
RESTORE DATABASE AdventureWorks2019_Test
FROM DISK = 'C:\Backups\AdventureWorks2019_Full.bak'
WITH MOVE 'AdventureWorks2019' TO 'C:\Data\AW_Test.mdf',
     MOVE 'AdventureWorks2019_Log' TO 'C:\Logs\AW_Test.ldf',
     REPLACE;

-- Point-in-time restore
RESTORE DATABASE AdventureWorks2019_PITR
FROM DISK = 'C:\Backups\AdventureWorks2019_Full.bak'
WITH NORECOVERY, REPLACE;

RESTORE DATABASE AdventureWorks2019_PITR
FROM DISK = 'C:\Backups\AdventureWorks2019_Diff.bak'
WITH NORECOVERY;

RESTORE LOG AdventureWorks2019_PITR
FROM DISK = 'C:\Backups\AdventureWorks2019_Log.trn'
WITH STOPAT = '2024-01-15 14:30:00';

-- Verify backup before restore
RESTORE VERIFYONLY
FROM DISK = 'C:\Backups\AdventureWorks2019_Full.bak';
sqlServer.description:
SQL Server restore operations support:
                • Full database restores with optional file relocation
                • Point-in-time recovery using full, differential, and log backups
                • REPLACE option to overwrite existing database
                • NORECOVERY to keep database in restoring state for additional restores
                • VERIFYONLY to check backup integrity without restoring

                Key features:
                • Can restore to different file locations
                • Supports partial restores and online restores (Enterprise edition)
                • Built-in verification with CHECKSUM
                • Progress monitoring with STATS option

                Always verify backups before critical restores. Use REPLACE carefully - it overwrites existing databases. NORECOVERY keeps database offline for additional restores. Point-in-time recovery requires log backup chain. Consider STANDBY option for read-only access during restore.

postgres.code:
-- Restore from Custom Format (Recommended)
-- First, create empty database
createdb -U postgres adventureworks_restored

-- Restore using pg_restore
pg_restore -h localhost -U postgres -d adventureworks_restored \
           -v adventureworks_backup.custom

-- Restore from Directory Format (Parallel)
createdb -U postgres adventureworks_parallel
pg_restore -h localhost -U postgres -d adventureworks_parallel \
           -j 4 -v backup_directory/

-- Restore from Plain SQL
createdb -U postgres adventureworks_plain
psql -h localhost -U postgres -d adventureworks_plain \
     -f adventureworks_backup.sql

-- Restore specific schemas only
pg_restore -h localhost -U postgres -d adventureworks_partial \
           --schema=sales --schema=hr \
           adventureworks_backup.custom

-- Restore with table exclusions
pg_restore -h localhost -U postgres -d adventureworks_clean \
           --exclude-table=audit_log \
           adventureworks_backup.custom

-- Clean restore (drop existing objects first)
pg_restore -h localhost -U postgres -d adventureworks_clean \
           --clean --if-exists \
           adventureworks_backup.custom

-- List contents of backup without restoring
pg_restore --list adventureworks_backup.custom
postgres.description:
PostgreSQL restore process requires:
                • Database must exist before restore (use createdb)
                • Different restore tools for different backup formats
                • pg_restore for custom, directory, and tar formats
                • psql for plain SQL format backups

                Key features:
                • Parallel restore with -j option for directory format
                • Selective restore of specific schemas, tables, or data types
                • Can exclude specific objects during restore
                • --clean option drops existing objects before restore
                • --list shows backup contents without restoring

                Always create target database before restore. Use parallel restore (-j) for faster restore of large databases. Test restore procedures regularly. Use --clean carefully - it drops existing objects. For PITR, combine base backup with WAL replay.

concepts.keyDifference: Must create target database before restore. Different tools for different backup formats. No built-in point-in-time recovery - requires WAL replay. Parallel restore available with directory format. More granular control over what gets restored. Clean restore option to handle existing objects. Cannot restore to different file locations.

concepts.title: Recovery Models

sqlServer.code:
-- Check current recovery model
SELECT name, recovery_model_desc
FROM sys.databases
WHERE name = 'AdventureWorks2019';

-- Set to FULL recovery model
ALTER DATABASE AdventureWorks2019
SET RECOVERY FULL;

-- Set to SIMPLE recovery model
ALTER DATABASE AdventureWorks2019
SET RECOVERY SIMPLE;

-- Set to BULK_LOGGED recovery model
ALTER DATABASE AdventureWorks2019
SET RECOVERY BULK_LOGGED;

-- Transaction log backup (FULL/BULK_LOGGED only)
BACKUP LOG AdventureWorks2019
TO DISK = 'C:\Backups\AdventureWorks2019_Log.trn';

-- Truncate log in SIMPLE mode (automatic)
-- No manual log management needed in SIMPLE mode
sqlServer.description:
SQL Server recovery models control transaction log behavior:
                • FULL: Complete logging, supports point-in-time recovery
                • SIMPLE: Minimal logging, automatic log truncation, no PITR
                • BULK_LOGGED: Minimal logging for bulk operations, supports PITR

                Key characteristics:
                • FULL model requires regular log backups to prevent log growth
                • SIMPLE model automatically truncates log, minimal space usage
                • BULK_LOGGED provides performance benefits for large data loads
                • Recovery model affects backup strategy and storage requirements

                FULL recovery model is required for Always On Availability Groups. SIMPLE recovery model cannot recover to specific point in time. Switch to BULK_LOGGED temporarily for large data operations. Monitor log file growth in FULL recovery model. Log backups are required in FULL model to truncate transaction log.

postgres.code:
-- Check current WAL level
SHOW wal_level;

-- Check archive mode status
SHOW archive_mode;
SHOW archive_command;

-- View current WAL settings
SELECT name, setting, unit, context
FROM pg_settings
WHERE name LIKE 'wal_%' OR name LIKE 'archive_%'
ORDER BY name;

-- Enable WAL archiving (requires restart)
-- In postgresql.conf:
wal_level = replica                    -- Enable WAL for replication/PITR
archive_mode = on                      -- Enable archiving
archive_command = 'cp %p /archive/%f' -- Archive command
max_wal_size = 1GB                     -- Maximum WAL size before checkpoint
checkpoint_completion_target = 0.9     -- Checkpoint timing

-- Reload configuration (for some settings)
SELECT pg_reload_conf();

-- Force WAL switch and archiving
SELECT pg_switch_wal();
postgres.description:
PostgreSQL uses WAL (Write-Ahead Logging) with configurable levels:
                • minimal: Basic crash recovery only
                • replica: Supports read-only replicas and WAL archiving
                • logical: Supports logical replication

                Key concepts:
                • wal_level controls amount of information written to WAL
                • archive_mode and archive_command enable WAL archiving for PITR
                • WAL files are automatically rotated and can be archived
                • No equivalent to SQL Server's SIMPLE recovery model

                wal_level = replica is required for PITR and streaming replication. archive_command must be configured for WAL archiving. WAL files consume disk space until archived or cleaned up. Use pg_archivecleanup to manage archived WAL files. Monitor WAL generation rate and archive performance.

concepts.keyDifference: WAL level configuration vs recovery model. File-based archiving vs transaction log backups. No automatic log truncation - requires archiving setup. Configuration file changes vs ALTER DATABASE commands. More granular control over logging levels. WAL archiving is separate from database backups.

concepts.title: Point-in-Time Recovery

sqlServer.code:
-- Point-in-time recovery sequence
-- 1. Restore full backup with NORECOVERY
RESTORE DATABASE AdventureWorks_PITR
FROM DISK = 'C:\Backups\AdventureWorks_Full.bak'
WITH NORECOVERY, REPLACE;

-- 2. Restore differential backup with NORECOVERY
RESTORE DATABASE AdventureWorks_PITR
FROM DISK = 'C:\Backups\AdventureWorks_Diff.bak'
WITH NORECOVERY;

-- 3. Restore log backups to specific point in time
RESTORE LOG AdventureWorks_PITR
FROM DISK = 'C:\Backups\AdventureWorks_Log1.trn'
WITH NORECOVERY;

RESTORE LOG AdventureWorks_PITR
FROM DISK = 'C:\Backups\AdventureWorks_Log2.trn'
WITH STOPAT = '2024-01-15 14:25:30';

-- Alternative: Stop at specific LSN
RESTORE LOG AdventureWorks_PITR
FROM DISK = 'C:\Backups\AdventureWorks_Log2.trn'
WITH STOPATMARK = 'User_Transaction',
     STOPAT = '2024-01-15 14:25:30';

-- View backup history for planning
SELECT database_name, backup_start_date, backup_finish_date,
       type, first_lsn, last_lsn
FROM msdb.dbo.backupset
WHERE database_name = 'AdventureWorks2019'
ORDER BY backup_start_date DESC;
sqlServer.description:
SQL Server PITR requires a complete backup chain:
                • Full backup as the foundation
                • Optional differential backup (most recent before target time)
                • Sequential log backups up to the desired recovery point

                Key features:
                • STOPAT allows recovery to specific date/time
                • STOPATMARK enables recovery to named transaction marks
                • LSN (Log Sequence Number) provides precise recovery points
                • Must maintain unbroken log chain for PITR

                Requires FULL or BULK_LOGGED recovery model. Must have unbroken chain of log backups. Use NORECOVERY for all but the final restore. Plan recovery points before beginning restore sequence. Consider marked transactions for application-consistent recovery.

postgres.code:
-- Point-in-time recovery setup
-- 1. Base backup (while database is running)
pg_basebackup -D /backup/base_backup \
              -U postgres -v -P -W \
              -X stream

-- 2. Configure recovery (create recovery.conf in PG < 12)
-- Or add to postgresql.conf (PG 12+):

# Recovery settings
restore_command = 'cp /archive/%f %p'
recovery_target_time = '2024-01-15 14:25:30'
recovery_target_action = 'promote'

-- 3. Start PostgreSQL for recovery
-- Database will replay WAL files until target time

-- Alternative recovery targets:
# Recover to specific transaction ID
recovery_target_xid = '12345'

# Recover to named restore point
recovery_target_name = 'before_data_load'

# Recover to specific timeline
recovery_target_timeline = '2'

-- Create named restore points
SELECT pg_create_restore_point('before_data_load');

-- View current WAL position
SELECT pg_current_wal_lsn();

-- Check recovery progress
SELECT pg_is_in_recovery();
SELECT pg_last_wal_receive_lsn();
postgres.description:
PostgreSQL PITR combines base backup with WAL replay:
                • pg_basebackup creates consistent physical backup
                • WAL files contain all changes since base backup
                • recovery.conf (or postgresql.conf) controls recovery behavior
                • Database replays WAL files until reaching target point

                Key features:
                • Multiple recovery target types (time, XID, name, timeline)
                • Can create named restore points for easier recovery
                • Automatic promotion to read-write after recovery
                • Timeline concept handles recovery from recovery scenarios

                Requires WAL archiving to be properly configured. Base backup can be taken while database is online. WAL files must be available from base backup time to recovery target. Use pg_create_restore_point() for application-consistent recovery points. Monitor recovery progress with pg_is_in_recovery().

concepts.keyDifference: Physical backup + WAL replay vs backup chain restore. Automatic WAL replay vs manual restore sequence. Configuration file vs SQL commands for recovery settings. Named restore points vs marked transactions. Timeline concept for complex recovery scenarios. Can recover while database is running (base backup).
